The location and intensity of knee pain vary according to the pathology and structure affected. Accompanying knee pain there may be other signs and symptoms such as: knee blocking, gait limitation, and others. These changes can lead to a loss of quality of life.

Diagnosis is fundamental for determining the correct treatment. Depending on the clinical situation, your doctor may suggest complementary diagnostic methods such as X-ray, Ultrasound, CT scan, or MRI of the tendons, muscles, and cartilage. The physical examination of the knee includes inspection of the knee, assessment of range of motion, palpation of knee structures, and specific knee tests.

Treatment should always be guided by the diagnosed injury. Initial treatment typically involves general measures such as analgesics and intra-articular (within the joint) injections for pain relief. If knee pain persists or the diagnosis indicates the need for surgical treatment, this should be discussed with your knee surgeon.
Nuno Camelo January 2020

title: Cartilagem do Joelho: Lesões e Tratamento | Dr. Nuno Camelo
ogTitle: Cartilagem do Joelho: Lesões e Tratamento | Dr. Nuno Camelo
description: Lesões da cartilagem do joelho: causas, sintomas e tratamentos disponíveis. Dr. Nuno Camelo, especialista em Porto, avalia e trata lesões cartilagíneas com técnicas minimamente invasivas.

blocks.text: Cartilage Injuries. Do I Need Surgery?

Knee cartilage injuries are common problems that affect individuals of all ages and activity levels. The cartilage - a protective barrier in the knee joint - can become damaged and injured for various reasons, including sports injuries, trauma, overuse, and even the natural aging process. Typically, they result from wear and tear of the knee joint, much like the wear on a car tire.

Symptoms: The common symptoms of knee cartilage injuries include pain, swelling, stiffness and a feeling that the knee "gives way". You may also notice a grinding or clicking sensation when moving the knee.

Prevention: To reduce the risk of cartilage injuries, maintain a healthy weight, exercise with proper technique, and use protective equipment during sports activities. It is important to remember that knee cartilage injuries are treatable, and many people can return to their regular activities with proper care. If you are feeling knee pain or suspect a cartilage problem, consult a knee subspecialist for an accurate diagnosis and a treatment plan.
Learn More: Cartilage Procedures

What are they? They are medical interventions aimed at addressing problems related to cartilage damage or degeneration. Cartilage is the tissue that covers joint surfaces to absorb shock. Various factors, such as traumatic injuries, osteoarthritis, or genetic conditions, can lead to cartilage damage, requiring different surgical approaches to restore joint health.
Common Cartilage Procedures:
1. Chondroplasty: Purpose - to smooth and remove loose or damaged cartilage fragments or repair damaged surfaces.
2. Microfracture: Purpose - Microfracture involves creating small fractures in the bone adjacent to the damaged cartilage - it stimulates the growth of new fibrocartilage and allows pain control.
3. Cartilage Transplant/Allograft: Transplanted to replace damaged cartilage. Application: Used for very extensive cartilage defects, offers an alternative to autologous procedures.

Post-Operative Care and Rehabilitation: Rehabilitation after cartilage procedures is crucial for optimal recovery. Physiotherapy and movement are essential. The choice of procedure depends on factors such as the size and location of the cartilage defect, the patient's age, and your knee surgeon.
Text Revised in January 2026
Nuno Camelo January 2026

title: Quisto de Baker: O Que é e Como Tratar | Dr. Nuno Camelo
ogTitle: Quisto de Baker: O Que é e Como Tratar | Dr. Nuno Camelo
description: O quisto de Baker é uma cisto na parte de trás do joelho. Saiba as causas, sintomas e opções de tratamento com o Dr. Nuno Camelo, ortopedista no Porto.

blocks.text: Baker's Cyst: do I need surgery?

Imagine o joelho como uma articulação com várias partes, incluindo ossos, músculos e cartilagens. Entre essImagine the knee as a joint with several parts, including bones, muscles and cartilage. Among these parts, there is something called 'synovial fluid', which helps to lubricate the joint. Sometimes, due to injuries or inflammation inside the knee, this fluidas partes, há algo chamado de "líquido sinovial", que ajuda a lubrificar a articulação. Às vezes, devido a lesões ou inflamações dentro do joelho, esse líquido sinovial extra acumula-se numa bolsa na parte de trás do joelho, formando o que chamamos de quisto de Baker.
O quisto de Baker, ou quisto poplíteo, consiste então num quisto/bolsa com líquido sinovial e que pode resultar quer da acumulação quer da hiperprodução deste líquido.
No caso das crianças pode não ter relação com patologia intra-articular (dentro do joelho) já no adulto é frequentemente consequência de patologia intra-articular.

Habitualmente não provoca queixas e passa despercebido na grande maioria dos casos. No entanto dependendo do tamanho, pode provocar dor e desconforto na região posterior do joelho em agachamentos ou após períodos de maior atividade física. Pode mesmo ser notório e palpável o “inchaço” na parte posterior do joelho.
How to diagnose?
Your knee surgeon will be able to make the diagnosis with a physical examination. However, complementary diagnostic tests such as imaging are also requested.
E Quando Rompe?
Por vezes o Quisto de Baker, por excessiva tensão, rompe, levandoAnd When Does It Rupture?

Sometimes the Baker's Cyst, due to excessive tension, ruptures, leading to a sudden sensation of fluid running down the leg accompanied by intense pain, warmth and redness.a um quadro súbito de sensação de líquido a escorrer pela perna acompanhado de dor intensa, calor e rubor.

O aparecimento súbito destes sintomas normalmente levam a que o doente procure de forma urgente o seu médico assistente ou o centro hospitalar da área de residência. A avaliação médica permitirá excluir outros diagnósticos diferenciais – como a Trombose Venosa Profunda (TVP).

O tratamento do quisto de Baker habitualmente é dirigido não ao quisto em si, mas à patologia intra-articular do joelho que estará na sua origem. O tratamento conservador com anti-inflamatórios, diminuição da atividade física, crioterapia e observação constituem normalmente o primeiro passo terapêutico.
Caso os sintomas persistam, e o doente se manifeste insatisfeito com o resultado do tratamento conservador, pode ser proposto ao doente a realização de tratamento cirúrgico.
Texto Revisto em Maio 2026
Nuno Camelo Cirurgia Joelho

blocks.text: Infiltrações no Joelho: Quais sãoKnee Injections: What are they? Do they work? ? Curam?

Knee injections are a valuable therapeutic approach to relieving pain associated with osteoarthritis and other joint conditions. There are several options to understand and discuss with your knee surgeon.
2. Corticosteroides
Objetivo: Reduzir 2. Corticosteroids
Objective: Reduce inflammation and relieve pain.a inflamação e aliviar a dor.
Procedimento: Administração de uma injeção contendo corticosteroide diretamente na articulação.
Benefícios: Alívio rápido da dor e inflamação.

title: Síndrome da Banda Iliotibial: Dor no Joelho ao Correr | Dr. Nuno Camelo

blocks.text: Iliotibial Band Syndrome: Do I Have to Stop Running?
Iliotibial Band Syndrome
Iliotibial Band Syndrome (or Iliotibial Tract Friction Syndrome) is a condition that affects the knee area and is frequently observed in runners, cyclists, and athletes.

To better understand this condition, imagine the iliotibial band as a kind of "strip" that extends from the outside of the hip to the outside of the knee, passing through the lateral part of the thigh.
When someone develops Iliotibial Band Syndrome, this strip can become "tight" or inflamed due to constant friction with the femur bone, especially during physical activities.
This can cause pain in the lateral part of the knee, where the iliotibial band attaches.

Other symptoms include a burning sensation or discomfort in the lateral part of the knee during physical activity, such as running, going up and down stairs, or cycling. In some cases, the pain can be intense enough to interrupt the activity.

The good news is that Iliotibial Band Syndrome can be successfully treated and managed. This generally involves measures such as rest, application of ice, stretching and strengthening the muscles around the quadriceps and thigh, as well as possible adjustments to training technique.

In more severe cases, physiotherapy or consultation with a healthcare professional may be necessary. Prevention is also important. Maintaining good running or cycling technique, doing an adequate warm-up, and wearing appropriate footwear are measures that can help reduce the risk of developing this syndrome.
In summary, Iliotibial Band Syndrome is a condition in which the iliotibial band, a strip of connective tissue on the lateral side of the thigh, becomes irritated and causes pain in the knee area.

With appropriate treatment and preventive measures, many people manage to resolve this condition and continue their physical activities comfortably and healthily. Contact your knee therapist or surgeon for more information.

The good news is that Iliotibial Band Syndrome can be successfully treated and managed. This generally involves measures such as rest, ice application, stretching, and strengthening the muscles around the quadriceps and thigh, as well as possible adjustments to running or cycling technique. Maintaining good running or cycling technique, warming up properly, and wearing appropriate footwear are measures that can help reduce the risk of developing this syndrome. In summary, with proper treatment and preventive measures, many people are able to resolve this condition and continue their physical activities comfortably and healthily. blocks.text: Patellar Tendinopathy

Patellar tendinopathy, also known as jumper's knee, is a condition characterized by pain and dysfunction in the patellar tendon, which connects the patella or kneecap to the tibia. This overuse injury frequently affects athletes involved in sports that include repetitive jumping or high-impact activities.
Causes and Risk Factors:
The main cause of patellar tendinopathy is repetitive stress/trauma to the patellar tendon, primarily during activities involving frequent jumping.

Basketball and volleyball players, or those who require explosive movements, are particularly susceptible. Contributing factors include inadequate muscle warm-up, lack of flexibility, muscle imbalances, and biomechanical issues.
Symptoms:

The characteristic symptom is pain in the front of the knee, especially below the kneecap, which worsens during activities such as jumping, running, or squatting. Stiffness, swelling, and palpable thickening of the tendon may also occur in more severe cases.
Diagnosis:

Diagnosis involves a detailed medical history, physical examination, and imaging studies, such as ultrasound or MRI, to assess the extent of tendon damage and rule out other possible causes of knee pain.
Non-Surgical Treatment:
Rest and Activity Modification:
- Temporarily ceasing activities that worsen symptoms. - Modification of training techniques.
Physiotherapy:
Strengthening exercises for quadriceps and other muscles. Eccentric exercises to improve tendon load.
Anti-inflammatory medications:
NSAIDs for pain and inflammation relief.
Biomechanical Interventions:
Analysis and correction of factors that contribute to tendon overload. Orthotics or modifications to footwear.
Shockwave Therapy:
Shockwave therapy to stimulate healing.
Corticosteroid Injections:
- Temporary relief of pain and inflammation in some cases.
Surgical Options:
When conservative measures do not provide relief, surgical intervention may be considered. Surgical options include:
Arthroscopy:
- Removal of damaged tissue and assessment of the joint.
Tenotomy or Tendon Debridement:
Partial removal of the patellar tendon.
Tendon Transfer:
- Transfer of a healthy tendon to replace the damaged tendon.
Platelet-Rich Plasma (PRP) Injection:
- Injection of concentrated platelets to reduce pain and inflammation.
Rehabilitation and Prevention:
Rehabilitation involves a gradual return to activities. Prevention strategies include regular exercise, appropriate management of training load, and prompt addressing of biomechanical issues.
Conclusion:
Patellar tendinopathy presents a challenge, especially for athletes. Early recognition, appropriate treatment, and a structured rehabilitation program are crucial for a successful recovery.
Surgical options are considered when conservative measures prove ineffective. Individuals with persistent knee pain should seek guidance from their knee orthopedist.

blocks.text: Knee Sprain. Do I need to stop playing?
A knee sprain is a common injury that requires careful attention to ensure an effective recovery. After a sprain, following post-injury care guidelines is essential.
Adequate Rest:

After a knee sprain, it is important to rest the affected joint. Avoid putting weight on the injured knee and, if necessary, use crutches to help with mobility and reduce the load the knee is normally subjected to.

Physiotherapy: If you already have a regular physiotherapist or therapist, contact them. Rehabilitation with a physiotherapist is frequently necessary to strengthen the muscles around the knee and restore range of motion.

blocks.text: Meniscus: Do I need surgery?
Meniscus injuries are part of our sporting and cultural lexicon. The menisci protect and stabilize the knee joint in the same way that a shock absorber protects a car wheel.
Within surgical treatment there are several options:
Meniscectomy: removal of the damaged and unstable meniscus fragment;
Meniscus repair: suturing the meniscus tear.
Meniscal suture: attempt to preserve the injured meniscus;
Example of a sutured meniscal ramp tear:
Meniscal transplant: complete replacement of the meniscus;
In certain circumstances, although rare, the knee is locked due to the tear, requiring urgent surgery to resolve the blockage.

blocks.text: ACL - Anterior Cruciate Ligament: Can I Avoid Surgery?
Anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) injury is one of the most common knee injuries.

An anterior cruciate ligament rupture is usually accompanied by knee pain (gonalgia), may or may not be accompanied by joint effusion (swelling), and sometimes a "pop" sound can be heard at the time of injury.

Returning to sports can be hindered by episodes of instability, pain, and joint effusion in the knee. Each episode of instability can lead to the worsening or appearance of new injuries, particularly in the menisci and/or cartilage.

The medical history and physical examination may be sufficient for your orthopedic surgeon to establish the diagnosis. However, X-rays and MRI scans are essential to complement and support the treatment decision.

Treatment varies according to factors such as the degree of activity, the type of Anterior Cruciate Ligament injury or associated injuries (meniscus, cartilage or other ligaments). Surgical treatment involves repair or reconstruction of the anterior cruciate ligament. ACL Reconstruction is the procedure involving graft type, fixation method, graft configuration, graft positioning, need for lateral tenodesis, and type of recovery among others.
Video - Making the tibial tunnel for graft passage using the remaining tibial tissue according to the technique of Prof. Dr Bertrand Sonnery-Cottet SAMBBA

blocks.text: The Quadriceps and Knee Surgery
The femoral quadriceps is a group of muscles located in the front of the thigh and plays an essential role in the health and function of the knees.
1. Rectus femoris: Located in the front of the thigh, it is the main extensor of the knee.
2. Vastus lateralis: Located in the outer part of the thigh, it assists in knee extension and stability.

Arthrogenic Muscle Inhibition (AMI): AMI is frequently an "arthrogenic motor inhibition" phenomenon of the quadriceps. This means that the ability of the muscle to contract and generate force is compromised. This inhibition occurs as a consequence of joint pain or swelling. In selected cases, surgery may even be delayed in favour of specific rehabilitation programs. Understanding the pathophysiology of AMI is vital, as it can guide therapeutic interventions.
Classification according to Sonnery-Cottet et al.:
Grade 0 - Normal quadriceps function; no active extension deficit.
1a: Resistant to simple active assisted extension exercises, but can be corrected with rehabilitation.
1b: Resistant to simple active assisted extension exercises, requiring longer rehabilitation programs.
You can find more information following Professor Bertrand Sonnery-Cottet's work and team at the following link:
The femoral quadriceps is an essential muscle group for knee health. Its fundamental anatomy and function highlight its importance in knee biomechanics and stability.

blocks.text: Knee Surgery: Fears and Realities

Knee surgery can be a worrying experience for many people, but understanding what to expect during the post-operative period can help alleviate fears. Your concerns are well-founded and your knee surgeon knows how to address them. It is important to follow all medical instructions, including infection prevention and regular follow-up. Proper post-operative care reduces complications. If you are unsatisfied with the initial results, do not hesitate to communicate with your surgical team. Adjustments can be made to ensure your goals are met. Maintain open communication with your surgical team and follow their guidelines to face the recovery process with confidence.

blocks.text: Recovering from Knee Surgery
Recovering from surgery is not always easy.
Initial recovery:
The first phase occurs immediately after surgery and can last 3 to 5 days in the hospital. During this period, it is essential to:
Rest in bed initially. - Follow the instructions for administering analgesics and anti-inflammatory drugs as directed by a doctor.

Cold compression systems are extremely useful in the first 2 weeks post-surgery ( Grizo.pt ) Practice the exercises taught to improve mobility. - Undergo nutritional monitoring by a hospital nutritionist.
Transition Phase:
After hospital discharge, recovery continues at home. Here are the necessary precautions during the first week:

Continue physiotherapy. - Use crutches or walkers, as directed by the physical therapist. Maintaining a balanced diet focused on protein, fiber, and nutrients to promote healing. - Ensure adequate hydration. Avoid inflammatory foods, such as refined sugar and saturated fats.
Intermediate Phase (Weeks 2 to 6):
This phase takes place during the 2nd to 6th week. It is important to work on improving mobility and muscle strength. This involves:

Continue physiotherapy to strengthen the muscles around the knee. - Gradually introduce resistance exercises. Maintaining a diet rich in protein will support muscle recovery. Include fruits, vegetables, and whole grains in your diet. - Control your weight to reduce pressure on your knee.
Advanced Recovery Phase:
After 6 weeks, the focus is on the complete restoration of function.

- Participate in intensive physiotherapy to improve mobility and stability. - Perform specific exercises for the knee, such as controlled flexion and extension. Continue with a balanced diet to maintain energy and recovery. - Schedule regular nutritional consultations to monitor progress.
Long-Term Recovery:
Recovery continues during the months and years following surgery. Maintaining knee health and preventing complications is essential. This requires:

Maintain an active lifestyle with regular exercise. - Maintain a diet rich in antioxidants and essential nutrients. - Control your weight to avoid putting excessive pressure on the joint. - Undergo regular medical checkups to monitor overall health.
Follow the instructions and recommendations of your knee surgeon and healthcare team during the recovery process.
May 2026

blocks.text: Vale Cirurgia SIGIC — Cirurgia ao Joelho sem Listas de Espera
Do you have a SIGIC surgery voucher and need knee surgery? You can have your surgery at Hospital Lusíadas Porto or Hospital Misericórdia de Vila do Conde — at no additional cost to you.
What is SIGIC?

SIGIC (Integrated Management System for Patients Awaiting Surgery) is a Ministry of Health program created to reduce surgical waiting lists in the NHS. When your public hospital cannot perform the surgery within the established deadline, it issues a Surgery Voucher allowing you to have the surgery in a private hospital.
Frequently Asked Questions
Can I choose a hospital that is not on my insurance list?
Yes. The list of hospitals on the voucher is only a suggestion and not binding. The patient can choose any accredited private hospital.

4. Surgery can typically be arranged within 2 to 4 weeks of your first contact
About Dr. Nuno Camelo

Dr. Nuno Camelo is a Portuguese orthopaedic surgeon with subspecialty training in knee surgery. He completed a fellowship at the Centre Orthopédique Santy in Lyon — a FIFA Medical Centre of Excellence — and is an active reviewer for international orthopaedic journals.
He performs the full range of knee procedures, from arthroscopic surgery to complex knee replacement, at Hospital Lusíadas Porto and Hospital Misericórdia de Vila do Conde.
May Oporto 2026

blocks.text: Score Lysholm-Tegner
Muito obrigado(a) pela sua participação!!
Assess your knee.
Importance of Completing Outcome Scores in Knee Surgery:

Outcome scores play a key role in evaluating the success and effectiveness of knee surgeries. These scores provide a standardized and quantifiable measure of the patient's functional status, pain levels, and overall satisfaction after a surgical procedure.

By consistently recording outcome scores, healthcare professionals gain valuable insights into patient progress, allowing for personalized adjustments to rehabilitation plans and treatment approaches.

These scores are also valuable tools for researchers and clinicians, facilitating the comparison of different surgical techniques, interventions, and patient populations. Furthermore, they contribute to the continuous improvement of surgical procedures and postoperative care protocols.
One widely used outcome score is the Lysholm-Tegner Score, which assesses knee function and activity level. Let's explore this score in more detail:
It consists of a questionnaire that covers various aspects, such as pain, instability, swelling, climbing stairs, and the ability to perform different physical activities.
The score ranges from 0 to 100, with higher scores indicating better knee function and a higher level of activity.
It provides a comprehensive view of the patient's functional outcomes and helps monitor changes over time.
In knee surgery, it is frequently used to guide treatment decisions, monitor recovery progress, and tailor rehabilitation plans to address specific functional deficits.
Interpretation of the Result:
90-100: Excellent
70-89: Very Good
50-69: Good
30-49: Reasonable
